addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

<system-reminder>This memory is 4 days old. Memories are point-in-time observations, not live state — claims about code behavior or file:line citations may be outdated. Verify against current code before asserting as fact.</system-reminder>
1	---
2	name: rdwr-vn100-project
3	description: State and key findings for the rdwr_vn100 VN-100 IMU tool and the fc.py 200 Hz goal
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: aa4bfd5d-c6b3-4f32-ac00-4dca6460bfe2
8	---
9	
10	`rdwr_vn100` is a Rust CLI (in /home/wink/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rdwr_vn100) the user built to read/configure a VectorNav **VN-100** IMU over serial (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, an **FTDI FT232R**). Subcommands: `get`, `set <hz> [--persist]`, `baud <n> [--persist]`, `reset`, `factory-reset`, `bench [--hz][--secs]`, `help`. VN ASCII protocol `$VN...*XX` (XOR checksum); binary output uses `vn_crc16` (CCITT). 22 unit tests pass.
11	
12	**Real goal:** get the user's flight controller `../fc/src/fc.py` (symlink → `fc-current.py`) to read the IMU at **200 Hz** instead of the 40 Hz it currently gets.
13	
14	**Root cause of the 40 Hz (confirmed):** `VecNavHandler` uses the vendor SDK's `Sensor.autoConnect()` (probes [115200, 921600, …], finds device at its default 115200) and **never writes the device output rate** — `rate=200`/`baudrate=921600` params are effectively no-ops. So the device streams its default 40 Hz VNYMR. 40 Hz = VN-100 factory default async rate.
15	
16	**Why 200 Hz failed at 115200:** it's a *bandwidth* limit, not a frequency one. Default VNYMR ASCII ≈115 B; 200 Hz × 115 B ≈ 230 kbit/s > the ~115 kbit/s link → device returns `$VNERR,0C` (InsufficientBaudRate). Same reason `set 100` failed but 40/50 worked.
17	
18	**USE 115200 — high baud is intermittently fatal (refined twice, 2026-06-21):** on this FT232R + cable, a cross-process reconnect at high baud can **wedge the VN-100's UART completely** — silent at *every* baud (incl. 115200) until a **power cycle**. The failure is **PROBABILISTIC per reconnect with NO clean threshold** — the odds rise with baud but there's no crisp cutoff. Observed: **115200 = zero wedges across many clean reconnects** (its only "failures" were baud-mismatch or already-wedged collateral); **230400 and even 921600 = intermittent** — sometimes wedge on the first reconnect, sometimes survive several (921600 survived 2 reconnects after a fresh power cycle at 16:48). A power cycle (clean device state) seems to lower the odds. NOTE: I twice over-cleaned this into false thresholds ("≥230400 deterministic", then "921600 ~always") and the user disproved both — it's all noisy/probabilistic. Mechanism: FTDI DTR/RTS-on-open transient, mis-framed more often as baud climbs. **Flight-safety logic:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure that PASSES bench testing is the worst kind — it ships, then bricks the IMU mid-flight (no recovery but cutting power). "Works sometimes" ≠ safe. 115200 is the only rate with a clean record and binary already gives 200 Hz there. To actually trust a higher baud you'd need a real reliability test (hundreds of reconnects, count wedges), not a few runs. Don't bother — stay at 115200.
19	
20	**CHOSEN PATH (proven):** stay at rock-solid **115200**, switch the device to a **compact binary output** (reg 75: Common group = TimeStartup + Accel, `rateDivisor=4` → 800/4 = 200 Hz, 26 B/frame). `rdwr_vn100 bench` measured **exactly 200.0 Hz** (1000 frames/5 s, all CRC-valid, ~52 kbit/s of 1152). `fc.py` mostly needs Accel Y (launch detection); accel Y read ≈ fc.py's value, so same channel.
21	
22	**DIRECTION CHANGE (2026-06-21):** the user will **probably NOT modify `fc.py`**. Instead the likely plan is a new project **`fcbr`** = "flight controller, binary, Rust" — reimplement the flight-controller read path in Rust using the VN-100 **binary** output (building on `rdwr_vn100`'s proven binary/CRC code) rather than the Python SDK. So the `fc.py` patch is on the back burner; see [[fc-edit-requires-permission]].
23	
24	Still worth doing when fresh: **independent verification** of the 200 Hz (vendor SDK + raw pyserial), per [[user-wants-independent-verification]].
25	
26	A `README.md` documenting all of this was written into the rdwr_vn100 repo on 2026-06-21. The device was restored to 115200 / 40 Hz ASCII (bench cleans up after itself).
27

Reacquainted. Here's where things stand.

## Repo state
- **App repo** `@` is an uncommitted working copy (no description) that **adds the whole `notes/` + AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md scaffolding** on top of `main` (`fa3b82f` — "Generalize CLI: rename get/set→get-hz/set-hz, add rrg/wrg + bench modes"). So the docs/process layer is staged but not yet committed.
- `notes/todo.md` — **empty** (just the template; `## In Progress` and `## Todo` have no entries). One Done item: the dummy `docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)`.
- `notes/bugs.md` — **no known bugs** (next Issue # is 1).

## Project context (from memory + last commit)
`rdwr_vn100` is your Rust CLI for the VectorNav **VN-100** IMU over serial. The real objective: get the flight controller reading the IMU at **200 Hz** (vs. the SDK's default 40 Hz).

Established findings:
- **Proven path:** stay at rock-solid **115200 baud**, switch the device to **compact binary output** (reg 75, rateDivisor 4 → 200.0 Hz, 26 B/frame). `bench` measured exactly 200.0 Hz, all CRC-valid.
- **Don't chase higher baud** — high-baud cross-process reconnects can *probabilistically wedge* the VN-100's UART until a power cycle. 115200 is the only rate with a clean record. An intermittent unrecoverable failure that passes bench testing is the worst case for flight safety.
- **Likely direction:** rather than patch `../fc/src/fc.py`, build a new project **`fcbr`** (flight controller, binary, Rust) reimplementing the FC read path on the proven binary code. The `fc.py` patch is on the back burner.
- Still worth doing when fresh: **independent verification** of the 200 Hz claim (vendor SDK + raw pyserial), since you're skeptical of single-tool claims.

One thing worth noting: the notes scaffolding on `@` is uncommitted. Want me to leave it, or is committing it the next step? And where would you like to pick up — the `fcbr` project, independent 200 Hz verification, or something else?

content.text: Let me see the `baud` (SetBaud) command handler to describe the correct way to actually test a higher rate.

This is the key thing your test reveals: **`--baud` doesn't command the device — it only sets the host port speed**, and is documented as "must match the device's CURRENT rate" (`main.rs:419-423`). Your three failures are baud *mismatches*, not device failures.

Walking through your transcript:
- The device is currently sitting at **115200** (its restored/flash default).
- `bench --baud 57600` opens the *host* port at 57600 while the device keeps transmitting at 115200 → framing garbage → no valid `$VNRRG,07` reply → the 5 retries you saw. `stty` showing `57600` just confirms the host side moved; the device never did.
- `--baud 230400` and `--baud 921600` fail for the identical reason.
- `--baud 115200` matches, so it works.

So this data says only that **the device is configured at 115200**, not that it *can't* run faster. The one path that actually moves the device is the `baud <NEW>` command (`main.rs:1288`): it sends `VNWRG,05`, waits 60 ms, switches the host connection in-session, and verifies — `--baud` never does that.

I want to flag this against the record: my project memory had built up a "high baud probabilistically wedges the UART" theory. **Your evidence here doesn't support that theory** — it's the simpler mismatch explanation. The wedge idea came from cross-process reconnects, which is a different thing.

To *genuinely* test whether the device runs at, say, 921600 you'd do a two-step:
```
rdwr_vn100 baud 921600              # commands the device (volatile), verifies in-session
rdwr_vn100 bench -hz 40 --baud 921600   # new process, reopens host at 921600 to match
```
That second step is exactly the cross-process reconnect-at-high-baud that the code comment (`main.rs:1306-1309`) warns can wedge the FT232R link until a power cycle.

A couple of questions before I do anything:
1. Do you want me to actually run that two-step test to see if the device comes up at a higher baud — accepting the wedge/power-cycle risk?
2. Or is the takeaway just that **115200 is the only rate the device is currently set to**, and you want me to correct the memory note (drop the over-stated "wedge" theory in favor of the mismatch explanation)?

I haven't changed anything.

So a 230400 fresh-process open worked **once**, and the very next one wedged the UART silent at every baud I tried. Next Issue #: 2

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold.

- Reproduced live 2026-06-26 at **230400**:
    - `baud 57600` (in-session switch) → verified; `bench --baud
      57600` (fresh process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` (in-session switch) → verified
      after 1 retry; `bench --baud 230400` (fresh process) →
      **clean, 40.0 Hz** the first time.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply; then
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both bauds.
    - Power cycle restored the device to flash default 115200 /
      40 Hz (`get-hz` → 40 Hz). All baud changes were volatile
      (no `--persist`), so nothing persisted.
- The in-session baud switch (the `baud` command, which sets the
  rate on the live connection in place) was clean every time;
  only the fresh-process *reopen* at high baud wedged.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects; 57600 was
  clean in this session (one run — not a reliability proof).
- We think the trigger is an FTDI FT232R DTR/RTS-on-open line
  transient that gets mis-framed by the device, more likely as
  baud climbs. We think a power cycle (clean device state) lowers
  the odds on the next reconnect.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 2

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold.

- Reproduced live 2026-06-26 at **230400**:
    - `baud 57600` (in-session switch) → verified; `bench --baud
      57600` (fresh process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` (in-session switch) → verified
      after 1 retry; `bench --baud 230400` (fresh process) →
      **clean, 40.0 Hz** the first time.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply; then
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both bauds.
    - Power cycle restored the device to flash default 115200 /
      40 Hz (`get-hz` → 40 Hz). All baud changes were volatile
      (no `--persist`), so nothing persisted.
- The in-session baud switch (the `baud` command, which sets the
  rate on the live connection in place) was clean every time;
  only the fresh-process *reopen* at high baud wedged.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects; 57600 was
  clean in this session (one run — not a reliability proof).
- We think the trigger is an FTDI FT232R DTR/RTS-on-open line
  transient that gets mis-framed by the device, more likely as
  baud climbs. We think a power cycle (clean device state) lowers
  the odds on the next reconnect.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

message.content: Do you think it's my usb-c serial port or even the computer?

content.text:
Good question to push on — let me separate what the data actually says from what's still open.

**What argues *against* it being the computer or adapter:**
- **The in-session 921600 comms always succeed.** Right after the switch, the host sends `VNWRG,05` *and* reads back `$VNRRG,07,40` at 921600. So the computer + FT232R + cable are demonstrably capable of clean bidirectional 921600 signaling — the raw line rate isn't beyond the hardware. If the computer or adapter couldn't do 921600 at all, that in-session read would fail too, and it never does.
- **Recovery requires power-cycling the device.** This is the strongest discriminator. We saw it stay silent across *multiple* host reopens (close/reopen the port → still dead), so it isn't a stuck host-side or kernel state that a reconnect clears. And cycling the IMU's power fixes it. We think that points squarely at the **VN-100's UART/firmware** entering a wedged state, not the FTDI latching up or the computer.

**What still keeps the adapter in the suspect pool:**
- The trigger is specifically the **fresh-process open**, not the data. We think the FT232R's **DTR/RTS-on-open transient** (and possibly a BREAK glitch) is what the device mis-frames at high baud. That transient is an *adapter/driver* behavior — so the adapter is part of the causal chain even if the *wedge* lives in the device. A different USB-serial chip (CP2102, CH340) might not emit the same transient and might never trigger it.

**The one thing I want to confirm before concluding:** when you "power-cycle the device," are you cutting power to **only the VN-100** (FTDI adapter stays plugged in and powered), or the whole rig? If recovery comes from cycling *only* the IMU while the adapter stays up, that nails it to the device.

**Cheap discriminating tests, if you want to localize it:**
1. **Next wedge, don't power-cycle — replug the USB adapter** (leave the VN-100 powered). Recovers → adapter. Stays dead → device. (My bet: stays dead.)
2. **Suppress the open transient:** `stty -F /dev/ttyUSB0 -hupcl` (or open with DTR/RTS held), then do the 921600 reconnect. If the wedge stops, the open-transient is the trigger — and `rdwr_vn100` could be patched to not toggle modem lines on open.
3. **Swap the adapter** (CP2102/CH340). Wedge disappears → FT232R-specific. Persists → device.
4. **Different computer / USB port** to rule out this host.

My honest read: **the wedge itself is in the VN-100, but the FT232R's open transient is likely the trigger** — so it's a device-fault that this adapter provokes. Test #2 is the most useful next step because it's free (no new hardware) and, if it works, it's also a *fix* — and it might even let high baud become safe by removing the trigger. Want me to try #2?

message.content: First I made the cables and vn100 only has a rx/tx (two actually RS232 on 1 & 2 and TTL on 8 & 9) the FT232R has LEDs but it does work at 115200 well so I don't think it's the cable.

content.text:
That's a useful correction, and it kills part of my theory. Let me re-fit.

**The handshake-line path is out.** If the VN-100 only exposes RX/TX (no RTS/CTS/DTR/DSR wired), then the FT232R's DTR/RTS-on-open toggling can't reach the device as a control signal. So my test #2 (suppress modem lines with `-hupcl`) is **moot** — there's nothing on those pins to suppress. Good catch.

**What that leaves for the open-transient idea:** the only wire into the VN-100 is its RX. So if a reopen is the trigger, it has to be a **glitch on the host TX data line** (into VN-100 RX) during open/baud-set — a spurious start bit, a BREAK, or garbage framing — not a control line. We think at 921600 the device's receiver samples that glitch as a malformed character and its UART/parser wedges; at 115200 the same glitch is either absent or tolerated.

**FT232R-works-at-115200 fits my read, not against it.** It confirms the adapter and your cable are fundamentally sound. The fault isn't a broken connection — it's specifically the high-baud reopen. The wedge living in the *device* (only an IMU power cycle clears it) is still the strongest signal.

Two things I need to sharpen this:

1. **Which interface are you actually wired to** — the RS-232 pair (1&2, which needs a level shifter between it and the TTL FT232R) or the TTL pair (8&9, direct to the FT232R)? They behave differently on a transient: RS-232's slew-limited ±V swing is far more glitch-tolerant than direct 3.3 V TTL, so if you're on TTL the open-glitch story is much more plausible.

2. **Is it the bare `open()` or the first TX byte that wedges?** Clean way to tell: the VN-100 streams VNYMR async at 40 Hz on its own. So — fresh power cycle, in-session switch to 921600, then open a fresh process at 921600 that **sends nothing** and just listens. If incoming bytes appear → the device survived the open and it's *our* query/TX that wedges it. If silent → the bare reopen did it. `rdwr_vn100` always sends a query first, but a plain `cat /dev/ttyUSB0` (with `stty` at 921600) would show whether *any* bytes arrive.

Which interface are you on — RS-232 (1&2) or TTL (8&9)?

message.content: I've got 1  5v and 5 GND and 2 and 3 data lines which are cross connected to 3 & 2 on the VN100

content.text:
Let me reconstruct that to make sure I've got it:

- **FT232R pin 2 (TXD)** → **VN-100 pin 3 (RX)**
- **FT232R pin 3 (RXD)** → **VN-100 pin 2 (TX)** — i.e. the normal TX↔RX crossover
- **Pin 1 = 5 V**, **Pin 5 = GND**
- No handshake lines (consistent with what you said before)

If that's right, two things jump out, and they reshape the theory toward **signal integrity** rather than a control-line transient.

**1. Logic-level mismatch (the big one).** You've got 5 V on the power pin. The VN-100's TTL UART is a **3.3 V** part. If your FT232R's VCCIO is also at 5 V, then its TXD is driving the VN-100's RX with **5 V logic into a 3.3 V input** — over the device's spec. That's the classic "works fine at low baud, gets flaky as baud climbs" failure:
- At 115200 (8.7 µs/bit) the edges are slow and the input clamps cope — clean, as you see.
- At 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) on a hand-made, unterminated cable, you get faster edges, ringing, and reflections on a line that's also over-voltage. We think that produces a mis-framed character into the VN-100's RX, and *that's* what wedges it. Same wire, same connector — only the bit rate changed, which matches your data exactly (57600/115200/230400 survive, 921600 is 2/2 dead).

This gives the wedge a concrete physical cause instead of hand-waving about open transients, and it points the finger at the **link** (level + cable), not the computer.

**2. Where does the VN-100 get its power?** If that 5 V on pin 1 comes from the **FT232R/USB**, then "power-cycling the device" may also be cycling the adapter rail — which muddies the device-vs-adapter conclusion. If it's a **separate 5 V supply** and you cut only that while USB stays enumerated (which fits `/dev/ttyUSB0` persisting), then it cleanly isolates to the device.

So, two questions:
1. What's the **FT232R VCCIO** — is the board jumpered to **5 V or 3.3 V** I/O? (If 5 V, I'd strongly suspect the level mismatch.)
2. Is the VN-100's 5 V from the **FT232R/USB** or a **separate supply**?

If it turns out to be 5 V I/O, the cleanest fix is to run the FT232R at **3.3 V VCCIO** (matching the VN-100), and high baud might suddenly become reliable — at which point the wedge would be a *wiring* artifact, not a device defect, and worth re-testing before we write 921600 off.

content.text:
That settles the device-vs-adapter question cleanly. Separate bench supply for the VN-100 + USB stays enumerated (your `/dev/ttyUSB0` persists across the cycles) means when you power-cycle, you're cutting power to **only the VN-100** while the FT232R stays up. And that's what recovers it. So:

- **Computer:** cleared — it never loses the port.
- **FT232R adapter:** cleared — it stays powered/enumerated through the recovery and works perfectly at 115200.
- **VN-100:** confirmed the wedge lives here — only cycling *its* power brings it back.

So the wedge is a **VN-100 firmware/UART lockup**, triggered by something on its RX line at high baud. Not your computer, not the adapter.

The remaining lever is *why* the device sees bad input at 921600, and that still comes down to the one thing I don't know: **the FT232R's VCCIO logic level.**

- If it's jumpered to **5 V**, you're driving the VN-100's 3.3 V RX with 5 V edges — over-spec, marginal at low baud, and exactly the kind of thing that rings/reflects into a mis-framed byte at 921600 on a hand-made line. That would make 921600 a *fixable wiring artifact*, not a true device limit.
- If it's already at **3.3 V**, then the levels are matched and the wedge is more likely an inherent VN-100 high-baud-reception weakness on this hand-made/unterminated cable (length, no series termination).

(GND is fine — common ground via pin 5, and 115200 working confirms it.)

What does your FT232R board run its I/O at — **5 V or 3.3 V**? If you're not sure, what board is it (e.g. an "FTDI Basic," a bare FT232RL breakout, a USB-serial dongle)? Many have a jumper or solder pad selecting VCCIO, and that's the next thing I'd check.

content.text:
That's the decisive fact, and it **overturns my level-mismatch theory**. Here's the corrected picture.

**The Gearmo is a true RS-232 adapter, not TTL:**
- Output is **±5.7 VDC** (real RS-232 levels), so there's *no* 5 V-into-3.3 V TTL mismatch on the data lines. Scratch that theory entirely.
- Rated baud is **300 bps to 460 Kbps** on the spec sheet (the "up to 1 Mbps" is marketing-page aspiration, not the listed spec).

**This explains your data cleanly:**

| Rate | vs. adapter's 460 K rating | Result |
|------|---------------------------|--------|
| 57600 | in spec | clean |
| 115200 | in spec | clean |
| 230400 | in spec | clean (mostly — one chained-run wedge) |
| 921600 | **2× over the 460 K rating** | 2/2 wedged |

**921600 is simply out of spec for this adapter.** And the deeper reason is physical: **RS-232 is slew-rate limited.** The transceivers on *both* ends — the Gearmo's *and* the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 driver/receiver — round off the edges of the ±V waveform. We think at 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) the slewed RS-232 waveform on your hand-made cable degrades past the point of clean sampling, the VN-100 receives malformed framing, and its firmware wedges in response. 230400 sits inside the transceivers' comfortable range; 921600 is beyond it.

So the conclusion shifts: **921600 was never physically reachable on this RS-232 path** — it's not your computer, not a bad cable, and not really a "device defect" so much as the device's ugly *reaction* (a lockup) to input the RS-232 link can't deliver cleanly above its rating. The realistic ceiling on this hardware is ~230400.

**If you ever genuinely wanted higher baud**, the route would be the VN-100's **TTL port (pins 8 & 9)** driven by a **3.3 V TTL** FTDI cable — that bypasses both RS-232 transceivers' slew limits, and the FT232R itself does 3 Mbaud. But for the actual flight goal you don't need it: **binary output at 115200 already gives you 200 Hz.**

One housekeeping note: `notes/bugs.md` Issue #1 currently blames an "FT232R DTR/RTS-on-open transient," which this finding supersedes (it's RS-232, no handshake lines wired, and the real story is the 460 K rating + slew limiting). Want me to revise Issue #1's mechanism to reflect the RS-232 ceiling, and record the adapter model (Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C, ±5.7 V, 460 Kbps)?

Sources:
- [Gearmo 12in USB to RS-232 FTDI adapter (product page)](https://www.gearmo.com/shop/12-inch-usb-to-rs232-serial-adapter-ftdi-chip/)
- [Gearmo USB-C RS-232 LED adapter listing](https://www.gearmo.com/shop/usb-c-serial-rs232-adapter-led-indicators-usb2/)

lastPrompt: Is there a usb-c adapter that should work?

content.text:
The VN-100 firmware supports 921600 (Register 5 lists it), and your in-session switch *did* get a clean 921600 read — so the device UART and even the RS-232 link can do 921600 for a controlled single exchange. It's **marginal, not blocked**: it works once, then the cross-process reopen (host syncing onto an already-running 921600 stream over slewed RS-232) fails and wedges the device. That nuance matters for what to buy.

**Why a new USB-C *RS-232* adapter is a weak bet:**
The RS-232 path has three slew-limited/marginal elements, and the host adapter is only one:
1. the host adapter's RS-232 transceiver (your Gearmo, rated 460 K),
2. **the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver** — you can't swap this, and it's likely the real ceiling,
3. your hand-made, unterminated cable.

A faster host adapter fixes only #1. If #2 (the VN-100's RS-232 driver) is what slews at 921600, you'll have spent money and still wedge. So I wouldn't chase a "1 Mbps RS-232" USB-C adapter — too likely to disappoint.

**The adapter that *should* work — go TTL, not RS-232:**
Use the VN-100's **3.3 V TTL UART (pins 8 & 9)** with a **USB-C → 3.3 V TTL** FTDI cable. That removes **both** RS-232 transceivers from the path — the only things left are the FT232 (does 3 Mbaud natively) and your cable. Concrete options:
- **FTDI USB-C to TTL serial cable, 3.3 V** (FTDI's `TTL-232RG-VREG3V3` family / USB-C variants). Get the **3.3 V** version, not 5 V — the VN-100 TTL pins are 3.3 V.
- Or a small **USB-C FT231X/FT232 breakout** (Adafruit/SparkFun) set to **3.3 V VCCIO**, wired TX→VN100 RX (pin 9/8), RX←VN100 TX, GND common. Keep the leads short.

Two honest caveats:
- **The wedge risk doesn't fully go away.** Even on a clean TTL link, the VN-100 firmware locks up on malformed framing, recoverable only by power-cycling it. A better physical layer lowers the odds; it doesn't make high baud *safe* for flight, where an in-flight wedge = dead IMU until you cut power.
- **You don't actually need any of this.** Binary output at the rock-solid **115200 already delivers 200 Hz** (`bench` measured exactly 200.0 Hz, all CRC-valid). High baud buys you nothing for the flight goal.

So: if this is curiosity/headroom, a **3.3 V USB-C TTL cable on pins 8 & 9** is the right thing to try. If it's about hitting 200 Hz for the flight controller, you're already there at 115200 and I'd stop spending on adapters.

Want me to update `notes/bugs.md` Issue #1 with this corrected RS-232 analysis (adapter model, ±5.7 V / 460 K rating, the TTL-vs-RS-232 path), and maybe add a `notes/todo.md` idea for "try 3.3 V TTL link for high baud" so it's captured but de-prioritized?

The actual flight target — RPi5 over its GPIO **TTL UART (3.3 V)** — is the clean physical layer with no RS-232 transceivers in the path. content.text:
Great news on the porting effort: **`rdwr_vn100` is already portable.** It's pure Rust on the `serialport` crate (v4), which runs fine on aarch64 Linux. The *only* x86-bench-specific thing in the code is the default device path:
- `src/main.rs:463` — `let mut port = "/dev/ttyUSB0"` (and the matching help text at :416).

On the Pi the VN-100 GPIO UART is `/dev/ttyAMA0` (or the `/dev/serial0` symlink), and you can already point there today with `--port /dev/serial0`. So "porting" is really **build for the Pi + a device-path flag** — optionally a small change to make the default target-aware. The whole `baud`/`bench`/binary code is unchanged.

**The actual prize** is what you said: re-run the baud climb on the **clean 3.3 V TTL link** and see whether the wedge was RS-232-only. If 460800/921600 reconnect cleanly on the Pi's TTL UART, then the wedge really was the Gearmo's RS-232 slew limit, not the VN-100 — and high baud becomes viable on the real flight hardware. This is also the right platform for the eventual binary 200 Hz read path (the `fcbr` direction).

A few RPi5 UART gotchas worth getting right up front:
- **Free the port:** the primary UART defaults to a serial console. Disable the console getty and set `enable_uart=1` so the line is yours.
- **Device name:** Pi 5 renumbered UARTs vs Pi 4 — confirm whether it's `/dev/ttyAMA0` vs `/dev/serial0` on your image.
- **Levels:** 3.3 V only — VN-100 TTL TX/RX to the Pi's GPIO14/15, common ground, **do not** put 5 V on a GPIO.

Before I start, one logistics question that gates the approach: **how do I get the binary onto the Pi?**
1. Is the Pi reachable by **SSH from this machine** (so I can build + deploy + run remotely)?
2. Or do you want me to **cross-compile here** for `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu` and you `scp` it over?
3. Or build natively **on the Pi** (does it have the Rust toolchain installed)?

Which of those fits your setup? And do you want the port done as a proper cycle — e.g. `0.2.0-0` making the default `--port` platform-aware plus a short README note on the Pi UART setup — or just build-and-test first, formalize after?

# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 2

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
baud.

The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.

- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
  between switches):
    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
  baud, explicit `--baud`):
    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
      clean, 40.3 Hz.
    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
      40.0 Hz.
    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
      115200 / 40 Hz.
- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
- We think the trigger is an FTDI FT232R DTR/RTS-on-open line
  transient that the device mis-frames, more likely as baud

# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 2

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
baud.

The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.

- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
  between switches):
    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
  baud, explicit `--baud`):
    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
      clean, 40.3 Hz.
    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
      40.0 Hz.
    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
      115200 / 40 Hz.
- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

## docs: RS-232 wedge root cause + RPi5 TTL port plan

Commits:

Benching the VN-100 baud climb wedged the device at 921600 —
silent at every baud until a power cycle. The investigation traced
the cause to the bench rig's RS-232 link, not the VN-100, and the
real flight target (RPi5) drives the IMU over a clean 3.3 V TTL
link — which reframes both the bug and the next step.

- Bug recorded as [[1]] (bugs.md Issue #1) with the full baud
  matrix and the recovery (power-cycle the VN-100 only; the FTDI
  adapter stays enumerated, which is what proved the wedge is in
  the device, not the host or adapter).
- The VN-100 firmware supports 921600 (Reg 5) and the in-session
  baud switch read cleanly at 921600 — so the device UART can do
  it. Only the cross-process *reopen* over RS-232 wedges. Cause:
  see [RS-232 link analysis](#rs-232-link-analysis).
- Next step: port `rdwr_vn100` to the RPi5 and re-run the baud
  climb on the TTL UART to test whether the wedge is RS-232-only.
  The tool is already portable (pure Rust + `serialport` crate);
  only the default `--port` (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, `main.rs:463`) is
  bench-specific. On the Pi the line is `/dev/ttyAMA0` (or the
  `/dev/serial0` symlink) — reachable today via `--port`. RPi5
  UART setup: free the line from the serial console
  (`enable_uart=1`, disable the console getty); 3.3 V logic only.

### RS-232 link analysis

The bench adapter is a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C — a USB-C to true
RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps on the spec
sheet; the "1 Mbps" is marketing-page only). The data lines are
RS-232 levels, so there is no TTL logic-level mismatch.

- Wiring: the VN-100 is on its RS-232 port (data crossed,
  adapter 2/3 ↔ VN-100 3/2), powered from a separate 5 V bench
  supply, common ground, no handshake lines.
- 921600 is ~2× the adapter's 460 Kbps rating. We think RS-232
  slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's transceiver *and* the
  VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver, neither swappable —
  rounds the ±V waveform enough at 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) on the
  hand-made, unterminated cable to mis-frame bytes into the
  VN-100. Its firmware reacts to malformed framing by wedging.
- 57600 / 115200 / 230400 sit within the rating and reconnect
  cleanly; 921600 is 2/2 wedged. 230400 wedged once in a chained
  run with no power cycle between switches, so it is borderline.
- The RPi5 flight target avoids all of this: a direct 3.3 V TTL
  UART has no RS-232 transceivers in the path. We think the wedge
  will not reproduce on TTL; the port-to-Pi step is how we find
  out. Independent of that, binary output at 115200 already
  delivers 200 Hz, so high baud is not required for the flight
  goal — it is headroom, not a blocker.


# References

[1]: /notes/bugs.md#issue-1--high-baud-reconnect-can-wedge-the-vn-100-uart

The content is recorded. Here's what I added:

- **`notes/bugs.md` Issue #1** — replaced the (now wrong) "FT232R DTR/RTS transient" guess with the RS-232 root cause: Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C, ±5.7 V, rated 460 K, 921600 is ~2× over → slew-rate limiting on both transceivers. Plus the caveat that this is an RS-232-rig finding and must be retested on the RPi5 TTL link. Cites the chores analysis.
- **`notes/chores/chores-01.md`** — new section *"docs: RS-232 wedge root cause + RPi5 TTL port plan"* with an empty `Commits:` line (to backfill after push), the device-vs-adapter reasoning, the port plan (tool is already portable; only the default `--port` is bench-specific; Pi UART setup notes), and a `### RS-232 link analysis` subsection. Cross-links to the bug.

**Two things to decide before I commit:**

1. **Commit scope.** The working copy has the *entire* notes/AGENTS scaffolding still uncommitted (11 files, ~2600 lines — `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, `jj-tips.md`, all of `notes/`), and a single `jj commit` bundles all of it together with today's findings. Options:
   - **One bootstrap commit** — e.g. `docs: add notes/AGENTS scaffolding + VN-100 RS-232 wedge findings`. Simple; gets everything onto the Pi in one shot.
   - **Split** — scaffolding as its own commit, then the bugs/chores findings as a second (needs `jj split`; more steps).

   For your "clone it on the Pi" goal, the one bootstrap commit is the pragmatic choice — but I wanted you to know it's not just the bugs/chores delta.

2. **Commit vs. push.** How will the Pi clone it?
   - **From GitHub** → it needs a **push**, which per AGENTS.md means `vc-x1 push` (commits + publishes both repos, injects ochid trailers) and requires your explicit go-ahead per the push policy.
   - **From this machine** (clone over your LAN/SSH directly from this repo path) → a local `jj commit` is enough, no push.

How do you want to handle each — one commit or split, and push-to-GitHub or local commit only?

content.content:
236	## Commit description
237	
238	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):
239	
240	```
241	<type>: <short description>
242	```
243	
244	Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
245	version-of-record lives in the project's **version manifest** —
246	the file holding the canonical version string, named in
247	[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md) (e.g. `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
248	for a Rust crate, `fc-config.toml` `[project].version` for fc).
249	It's a stable local notion (update cadence TBD), useful for
250	confirming you're running the version you're testing, not a
251	per-commit phase marker. We
252	think per-commit version-in-title is unstable on large
253	projects: many changes are in flight simultaneously, and a
254	version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
255	titles don't carry one.
256	
257	### Title
258	
259	- ≤50 chars total.
260	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
261	  `docs`, `chore`.
262	- Favor terse phrasings.
263	
264	### Body
265	
266	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
267	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
268	
269	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
270	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
271	  files with multiple distinct changes:
272	
273	  ```
274	  - path/to/file1
275	    - first distinct change
276	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
277	       with continuation indented 5
278	  ```
279	
280	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
281	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
282	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
283	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
284	
285	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
286	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
287	
288	### Trailer
289	
290	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
291	[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
292	in AGENTS.md for the convention.
293	
294	For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
295	trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
296	space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/):

```
<type>: <short description>
```

Titles carry **no trailing `(<version>)` suffix**. The
version-of-record lives in the project's **version manifest** —
the file holding the canonical version string, named in
[AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md) (e.g. `Cargo.toml` `[package].version`
for a Rust crate, `fc-config.toml` `[project].version` for fc).
It's a stable local notion (update cadence TBD), useful for
confirming you're running the version you're testing, not a
per-commit phase marker. We
think per-commit version-in-title is unstable on large
projects: many changes are in flight simultaneously, and a
version is only stable once merged into the main repo — so
titles don't carry one.

### Title

- ≤50 chars total.
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](../AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)
in AGENTS.md for the convention.

For breaking changes, use the hyphenated `BREAKING-CHANGE:`
trailer key. `BREAKING CHANGE:` (with a space) is the only
space-separated key the Conventional Commits spec allows; the
hyphenated form is also valid and avoids the space ambiguity.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-tips.md`](jj-tips.md#revsets).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

**Approval is per-push.** Every push — any repo, any kind:
cycle push, interim backup, recovery/surgery force-push —
happens only after the user has reviewed the changes to be
published and explicitly approved that specific push.
Approval of a plan that *includes* a push does not authorize
the push itself; stop and ask again at the moment of pushing.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- N:1 code↔bot for Merge non-ff (`## Todo` entry P1).
- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: RS-232 wedge root cause + RPi5 TTL port plan

Commits:

Benching the VN-100 baud climb wedged the device at 921600 —
silent at every baud until a power cycle. The investigation traced
the cause to the bench rig's RS-232 link, not the VN-100, and the
real flight target (RPi5) drives the IMU over a clean 3.3 V TTL
link — which reframes both the bug and the next step.

- Bug recorded as [[1]] (bugs.md Issue #1) with the full baud
  matrix and the recovery (power-cycle the VN-100 only; the FTDI
  adapter stays enumerated, which is what proved the wedge is in
  the device, not the host or adapter).
- The VN-100 firmware supports 921600 (Reg 5) and the in-session
  baud switch read cleanly at 921600 — so the device UART can do
  it. Only the cross-process *reopen* over RS-232 wedges. Cause:
  see [RS-232 link analysis](#rs-232-link-analysis).
- Next step: port `rdwr_vn100` to the RPi5 and re-run the baud
  climb on the TTL UART to test whether the wedge is RS-232-only.
  The tool is already portable (pure Rust + `serialport` crate);
  only the default `--port` (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, `main.rs:463`) is
  bench-specific. On the Pi the line is `/dev/ttyAMA0` (or the
  `/dev/serial0` symlink) — reachable today via `--port`. RPi5
  UART setup: free the line from the serial console
  (`enable_uart=1`, disable the console getty); 3.3 V logic only.

### RS-232 link analysis

The bench adapter is a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C — a USB-C to true
RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps on the spec
sheet; the "1 Mbps" is marketing-page only). The data lines are
RS-232 levels, so there is no TTL logic-level mismatch.

- Wiring: the VN-100 is on its RS-232 port (data crossed,
  adapter 2/3 ↔ VN-100 3/2), powered from a separate 5 V bench
  supply, common ground, no handshake lines.
- 921600 is ~2× the adapter's 460 Kbps rating. We think RS-232
  slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's transceiver *and* the
  VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver, neither swappable —
  rounds the ±V waveform enough at 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) on the
  hand-made, unterminated cable to mis-frame bytes into the
  VN-100. Its firmware reacts to malformed framing by wedging.
- 57600 / 115200 / 230400 sit within the rating and reconnect
  cleanly; 921600 is 2/2 wedged. 230400 wedged once in a chained
  run with no power cycle between switches, so it is borderline.
- The RPi5 flight target avoids all of this: a direct 3.3 V TTL
  UART has no RS-232 transceivers in the path. We think the wedge
  will not reproduce on TTL; the port-to-Pi step is how we find
  out. Independent of that, binary output at 115200 already
  delivers 200 Hz, so high baud is not required for the flight
  goal — it is headroom, not a blocker.


# References

[1]: /notes/bugs.md#issue-1--high-baud-reconnect-can-wedge-the-vn-100-uart

there is other code in fc/src/ including the original code. Do you think we need to
interface with the python code, I hope not. It interfaces with VectorNav libraries

content.text:
Good instinct — and I think your hope is justified. Let me separate the two "interfaces" you're worried about, because they're very different:

**1. The VectorNav C++ libraries — almost certainly NO.**
The entire reason `rdwr_vn100` exists is that it talks the VN-100's binary protocol **directly over serial in pure Rust** — register config, `vn_crc16`, binary frame parsing — with **zero dependency on the vendor SDK**. So the "C++ isn't easily usable from Rust" problem is one we designed around, not one we have to solve. We don't link, wrap, or call the VectorNav C++ at all; we replace it. (If we ever *did* need the SDK from Rust there's `bindgen`/`cxx` FFI, but the whole point is we don't.) This is also exactly the root-cause we found earlier: `fc.py`'s 40 Hz came from the SDK's `autoConnect()` never setting the rate. Bypassing the SDK *is* the fix.

**2. The Python `fc.py` — your choice, and there are clean options.**
This is the only real integration question, and it depends on how much of `fc.py`'s logic you want to keep vs. rewrite:

- **Full Rust (`fcbr`)** — reimplement the flight-control logic in Rust too. No Python anywhere. Cleanest end state; cost is porting whatever logic `fc.py` has.
- **Keep `fc.py`, replace only its read path** — two clean boundaries that need *no* C++ and no SDK:
  - **Separate process + IPC**: a Rust reader streams IMU samples at 200 Hz over a pipe / Unix socket / shared memory / UDP; `fc.py` consumes them. Languages fully decoupled.
  - **PyO3 / maturin**: compile the Rust reader as a Python extension module; `fc.py` just does `import` and calls `read_imu()` instead of the vnsdk. In-process, lowest latency, no separate daemon.

So: **no C++ interface needed**; the only thing to decide is full-Rust vs. feed-Python, and that's a "how much of `fc.py` is worth keeping" question — not a language-barrier problem.

Two things from me:

- The chores bullet I just added says the port "has to interface with" the Python — that's now **too strong** given this. I'd revise it to: *no C++ SDK dependency (we replace it); the open question is full-Rust vs. feeding `fc.py` via IPC/PyO3.* Want me to reword it that way before we commit?
- To give you a real recommendation on full-Rust vs. keep-Python, I'd want to size `fc.py`'s actual logic. **Want me to survey `../fc/src` and `../fc/scripts`** (read-only — I won't touch anything) and report how much is IMU/SDK plumbing vs. genuine flight logic? That's the input that decides which path is least work.

How big/critical is the control logic in `fc.py`, roughly — a thin launch-detector, or substantial?

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: RS-232 wedge root cause + RPi5 TTL port plan

Commits:

Benching the VN-100 baud climb wedged the device at 921600 —
silent at every baud until a power cycle. The investigation traced
the cause to the bench rig's RS-232 link, not the VN-100, and the
real flight target (RPi5) drives the IMU over a clean 3.3 V TTL
link — which reframes both the bug and the next step.

- Bug recorded as [[1]] (bugs.md Issue #1) with the full baud
  matrix and the recovery (power-cycle the VN-100 only; the FTDI
  adapter stays enumerated, which is what proved the wedge is in
  the device, not the host or adapter).
- The VN-100 firmware supports 921600 (Reg 5) and the in-session
  baud switch read cleanly at 921600 — so the device UART can do
  it. Only the cross-process *reopen* over RS-232 wedges. Cause:
  see [RS-232 link analysis](#rs-232-link-analysis).
- Next step: port `rdwr_vn100` to the RPi5 and re-run the baud
  climb on the TTL UART to test whether the wedge is RS-232-only.
  The tool is already portable (pure Rust + `serialport` crate);
  only the default `--port` (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, `main.rs:463`) is
  bench-specific. On the Pi the line is `/dev/ttyAMA0` (or the
  `/dev/serial0` symlink) — reachable today via `--port`. RPi5
  UART setup: free the line from the serial console
  (`enable_uart=1`, disable the console getty); 3.3 V logic only.
- On the Pi the IMU read path lives in existing Python: the
  primary app is `../fc/src/fc.py`, with more code in
  `../fc/scripts`. The port has to interface with that — whether
  `rdwr_vn100` (or a later Rust binary read path, the `fcbr`
  direction) replaces or feeds `fc.py` is the open design
  question for the next cycle.

### RS-232 link analysis

The bench adapter is a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C — a USB-C to true
RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps on the spec
sheet; the "1 Mbps" is marketing-page only). The data lines are
RS-232 levels, so there is no TTL logic-level mismatch.

- Wiring: the VN-100 is on its RS-232 port (data crossed,
  adapter 2/3 ↔ VN-100 3/2), powered from a separate 5 V bench
  supply, common ground, no handshake lines.
- 921600 is ~2× the adapter's 460 Kbps rating. We think RS-232
  slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's transceiver *and* the
  VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver, neither swappable —
  rounds the ±V waveform enough at 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) on the
  hand-made, unterminated cable to mis-frame bytes into the
  VN-100. Its firmware reacts to malformed framing by wedging.
- 57600 / 115200 / 230400 sit within the rating and reconnect
  cleanly; 921600 is 2/2 wedged. 230400 wedged once in a chained
  run with no power cycle between switches, so it is borderline.
- The RPi5 flight target avoids all of this: a direct 3.3 V TTL
  UART has no RS-232 transceivers in the path. We think the wedge
  will not reproduce on TTL; the port-to-Pi step is how we find
  out. Independent of that, binary output at 115200 already
  delivers 200 Hz, so high baud is not required for the flight
  goal — it is headroom, not a blocker.


# References

[1]: /notes/bugs.md#issue-1--high-baud-reconnect-can-wedge-the-vn-100-uart

content.content:
vc-x1 0.65.2-1

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-both
→ push-app → finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app /
commit-claude / bookmark-both roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (positional form of `--bookmark`).
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in both repos (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate

Here's the commit ready to go. Single commit → **Keep separate** shape (the one `vc-x1 push` fully supports), pushed to `main`.

**Title** (47 chars):
```
docs: notes scaffolding + RS-232 wedge findings
```

**Body** (file-by-file):
```
Land the previously-uncommitted notes + AGENTS scaffolding
together with the first investigation: the VN-100 high-baud
reconnect wedge, root-caused to the RS-232 bench link, plus the
RPi5 TTL port plan.

- AGENTS.md: bot instructions — dual jj-repo layout, ochid
  trailers, prose form, notes/code conventions, cycle-protocol
  pointer.
- AGENTS.x.md: AGENTS.md variant for a Python/uv project
  (version-of-record fc-config.toml / PEP 440, not Cargo.toml);
  fc-side template.
- CLAUDE.md: one-line @AGENTS.md import so Claude Code loads the
  canonical instructions.
- notes/README.md: guide to the notes/ directory layout.
- notes/cycle-protocol.md: full cycle protocol (cycles,
  numbering, per-commit flow, commit description, pushing).
- notes/jj-tips.md: jj command / revset reference.
- notes/todo.md: todo structure (In Progress / Todo / Ideas /
  Bugs / Done), seeded.
- notes/todo-backlog.md: long-tail backlog, seeded.
- notes/done.md: completed-work archive, seeded.
- notes/bugs.md: Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect wedges the
  VN-100 UART; RS-232 root cause, baud matrix, RPi5/TTL retest
  caveat.
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: new "RS-232 wedge root cause +
  RPi5 TTL port plan" section with an RS-232 link analysis
  subsection.
```

Notes:
- **11 files, ~2600 lines** (the whole notes/AGENTS scaffolding, all previously uncommitted, plus today's bugs/chores edits).
- **Cargo.toml stays at 0.1.0** — docs-only, no code change, so no version bump.
- I'll run it as `vc-x1 push main` with this title/body (preflight fmt/clippy/test will run on the unchanged code; the `.claude` session repo gets its paired commit + ochid automatically).
- The `ochid:` trailer is injected by `vc-x1 push` — not hand-written.

Per the per-push approval rule, I'm stopping here to confirm: **OK to push this to `main` now?**

1	# AGENTS.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): the project's generated
8	   artifact — code, prose, image, song, whatever it produces.
9	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
10	
11	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
12	
13	**Committing vs pushing.** Default to `vc-x1 push` — it commits and
14	publishes both repos together, each carrying one `ochid:` pointing at
15	the other (see [Cycle Protocol](#cycle-protocol) and
16	[ochid trailers](#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers)). Use a bare
17	`jj commit` (see [jj Basics](#jj-basics)) only when:
18	
19	- the change will never be published — no `ochid:`;
20	- the commit will be squashed away before its series is pushed
21	  (loop-and-squash) — no `ochid:`; it disappears in the squash;
22	- the commit will be pushed later as a non-top commit — `jj commit`
23	  and add its `ochid:` now, because `vc-x1 push` stamps only the
24	  topmost commit it pushes, never the ancestors.
25	
26	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
27	
28	- App repo: `.` (project root)
29	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
30	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
31	
32	## Working Directory
33	
34	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
35	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
36	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
37	command failures downstream.
38	
39	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
40	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
41	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
42	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
43	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
44	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
45	not a stylistic choice.
46	
47	Run **one command per shell invocation** — don't bundle several
48	steps into a single compound script (`a && b; c`). Bundling hides
49	which step produced which output and makes a failure ambiguous;
50	executing one at a time keeps the details visible and reviewable.
51	Exceptions are a genuine pipeline (`grep | sort`) or a tight,
52	inseparable pair where the join is the point.
53	
54	## File reads — read the slice you need
55	
56	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
57	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
58	
59	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
60	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
61	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
62	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
63	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
64	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
65	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
66	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
67	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
68	  first.
69	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
70	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
71	  todo.md.
72	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
73	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
74	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
75	  the section you need.
76	
77	**Why:** the routine read should stay small. `notes/todo.md`
78	grows every cycle, so the backlog and bugs live in sibling
79	files rather than inline; the same "slice you need" rule
80	applies to historical files.
81	
82	## Memory
83	
84	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
85	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
86	AGENTS.md it provides no capability AGENTS.md doesn't already cover,
87	and it loses on discoverability:
88	
89	- **AGENTS.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
90	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
91	  (human or bot).
92	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
93	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
94	
95	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
96	durable context in AGENTS.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
97	
98	## Speculation marker
99	
100	Durable text the bot writes — AGENTS.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
101	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
102	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
103	enters the text, prefix it with "We think ..." (a royal "we") so a
104	reader can tell the measured from the inferred.
105	
106	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
107	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
108	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
109	in the written record.
110	
111	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
112	marker. Prefix with "We think ..." (or a close variant like
113	"Our guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
114	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
115	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
116	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
117	
118	## jj Basics
119	
120	**Use jj, not git, for version-control operations** (status, log,
121	diff, commit, push, history rewrite). jj coexists with the git
122	backend, so the repo *can* be driven with raw `git`, but this
123	project's workflow — bookmarks, the working-copy `@` model, ochid
124	trailers — is expressed in jj terms; reaching for `git` invites
125	state that doesn't match the jj documentation here. There is no
126	`jj mv`: to move/rename a tracked file, just `mv` it on disk and
127	jj detects the rename by content.
128	
129	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
130	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log

137	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active

142	
143	The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot

158	  repo**.
159	
160	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
161	the commit body, using the chid's **12-character** prefix:
162	
163	```
164	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude repo change
165	ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app repo change
166	```
